voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
where_lives: North, Central, and South America — grasslands, forest edges, and scrubland
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: It smells insects living up to 20 centimeters below the soil surface.
Its hearing is good and it uses sound to detect approaching danger.
It stands on its hind legs and sniffs the air for nearby threats.
Its eyesight is poor and works best only at very close range.
Where does it live — and why?: It lives in grasslands, forest edges, and scrubland across the Americas.
Soft soil is essential — it digs burrows for sleeping and raising young.
It stays in areas with dense insect and invertebrate populations underground.
It needs access to water and avoids very cold climates.
How does it send messages?: Mating pairs make a soft chucking sound to communicate with each other.
A female's anal gland scent changes when she is ready to mate.
It stamps its feet and stands upright when alarmed.
It uses scent to recognize familiar places and navigate its home range.
What does it do when something changes?: When startled, it can jump straight up into the air before running.
It can swim across streams by swallowing air to help it float.
Near humans, it shifts its activity to nighttime to avoid contact.
Inside a burrow it arches its back to brace and resist removal.
How does it learn?: Young armadillos follow the mother and observe her foraging routes.
They learn which substrate types hold the most insects by following her digging pattern.
All four identical young develop together and learn the same home range.
How does it have babies?: A female always gives birth to exactly four identical young.
The young are born with soft, leathery shells that harden within weeks.
Newborns are small but have their eyes open and can walk shortly after birth.
Young stay in the burrow with the mother before foraging on their own.
What are its limits?: Cold weather slows it sharply; it has few fat reserves for winter.
Hard, rocky ground prevents it from digging the burrows it needs.
Very loud sounds or sudden disturbances cause it to freeze or flee erratically.
Its startle-jump response causes many deaths near roads and traffic.
What does this teach us?: A sensitive nose is more useful than eyes in its underground world.
Its bony shell records its growth over many years.
It shifts to night activity near humans, showing strong behavioral flexibility.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Always gives birth to four identical young from a single fertilized egg (monozygotic quadruplets).
Has poor eyesight but a highly sensitive nose for underground prey.
Can jump straight up when startled — a real, measurable reflex.
Can swim by swallowing air to increase buoyancy.
still_debated: How much its expanding range is climate-driven vs. human-landscape driven.
Whether the startle-jump reflex evolved for predator avoidance, vehicle avoidance, or both.
How strongly scent is used to mark re-used foraging areas.
common_failure_modes: Treating 'armadillo' as one species when there are about twenty.
Describing it as nocturnal when it frequently shifts based on disturbance.
Overstating leprosy transmission risk without context.
species_specific_guardrails: Do not generalize from Dasypus novemcinctus to the giant or three-banded armadillos.
Do not claim it 'rolls into a ball' — that's the three-banded species.
Do not describe its shell as pure bone; it is bone plus keratinous scutes.
